question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has any responsibility over the seizure and disposal powers for e-scooters, scramblers and Cars, involved in anti-social behaviour.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he is taking to reduce waiting times for those requiring surgery for hidradenitis suppurativa, in light of advice from the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust that would be some time before an appointment can be offered.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the rationale for treating Carer’s Allowance as income in discretionary support assessments, given that it is intended to recognise the additional and often unacknowledged responsibilities of unpaid carers and may result in families being excluded from vital support.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people who have presented at an Emergency Department following an accident on an e-scooter or scrambler, since 2024, broken down by injury.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of applications for discretionary support deemed ineligible due to carer's allowance taking them over the threshold, since 2022.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of temporary repairs currently in place across schools in North Belfast.
answer_text: The Education Authority does not maintain a central record of “temporary repairs” within schools. Maintenance activity is recorded through individual response and planned task orders and does not distinguish between temporary and permanent repairs. As such, it is not possible to provide a quantified figure for the number of temporary repairs currently in place across schools in North Belfast.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps that can be taken to ensure patients with hidradenitis suppurativa receive timely and appropriate treatment.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail how long patients with hidradenitis suppurativa are waiting for surgery.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support that can be made available to patients dealing with severe pain relating to hidradenitis suppurativa.
answer_text: Patients presenting to their GP with suspected hidradenitis suppurativa are clinically assessed to determine disease severity and underlying factors. Initial management, including antibacterial washes, antibiotics or non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, can be initiated in primary care. Pain management for this condition is guided by GP who starts initial treatment and if required additional therapies can be provided in secondary care. Where first-line treatment is ineffective, GPs may refer patients to dermatology services within their local HSC Trust. Referral priority is determined by the GP, based on clinical assessment, medical history, and response to previous treatments.All HSC Trusts provide assessment and treatment in line with best-practice guidance from the British Association of Dermatologists (BAD) and the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE). Treatment options may include biologic therapies, retinoids or surgery, where appropriate, to help manage symptoms and reduce the frequency and severity of flare-ups. Smoking in this population is a significant issue and smoking cessation services designed to help individuals quit smoking effectively, including support from trained advisers and access to nicotine replacement therapies are available across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has identified any communities that may be disproportionately affected by potential youth club closures due to funding uncertainty beyond June 2026.
answer_text: My department has not identified any communities that may be disproportionately affected by potential youth club closures due to funding uncertainty beyond June 2026. The Education Authority will continue to assess the needs of children and young people and ensure youth provision meets these as far as possible within the budget available to them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the current curriculum content relating to gender based violence and misogyny; and (ii) whether his Department has any plans to update the curriculum to more explicitly address these topics.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the composite answer to AQW 38707/22-27, AQW 38708/22-27, AQW 38709/22-27, AQW 38710/22-27 and AQW 38711/22-27 (provided to Claire Sugden MLA on 3 February 2026), which is contained in the weekly answer booklet w/e 6 February 2026.My Department is currently undertaking a programme of curriculum reform.  This includes revising existing content within the area of Learning for Life and Work,  providing an opportunity to set clearer curriculum expectations around respectful relationships and harmful behaviours in an age-appropriate way.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement his Department has had with (i) school principals; (ii) parents; and (iii) student organisations, regarding AI.
answer_text: Engagement with schools on Artificial Intelligence has primarily been through the Education Authority’s EdIS C2k programme, which provides the secure education technology infrastructure and learning environment used by schools.EA’s EdIS C2k team works directly with schools to support understanding of emerging technologies and their appropriate use in teaching and learning contexts, with principals routinely included in these engagements through established school communication channels.There has been regular engagement with school principals through structured communications, professional learning activities and events co-ordinated by EAs EdIS/C2k teams. Engagement with parents has primarily been indirect and focused on assurance around safety, safeguarding and equity of access. Parents receive information through school-led communications and through system-wide digital services provided by EdIS C2k. Further advice on AI has been supported through centrally co-ordinated events and communications, including Digital Skills Week, Safer Internet Day and online safety learning events delivered in partnership with recognised education technology partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has conducted any assessment of the impact of removing ring-fenced funding for youth services.
answer_text: My Department has not conducted an assessment of the impact of the decision in respect of the removal of the ring-fencing for the youth services budget.Historically, the budget for youth services has been a standalone budget within the overall Education budget, allocated by my Department to the Education Authority (EA) specifically to meet the statutory requirement to secure adequate facilities for youth activities.Funding for youth services has not been removed but rather, going forward, a single composite Block Grant allocation will be provided to the EA combining the budget allocations for both Schools and Youth services.  This will bring Youth funding into line with other established education budgets and will give the EA the flexibility to make appropriate, evidence-based decisions in respect of its budget in totality.  I would emphasise that all spending remains subject to policy, legislation and Departmental oversight, and the statutory requirement to secure adequate facilities for youth activities remains.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has carried out any assessment of how staffing levels within youth services may be affected by the removal of ring-fenced funding.
answer_text: No assessment has been carried out by my department of how staffing levels within youth services may be affected by the removal of ring-fenced funding. Historically, the budget for youth services has been a standalone budget within the overall Education budget, allocated by my Department to the Education Authority (EA) specifically to meet the statutory requirement to secure adequate facilities for youth activities.Funding for youth services has not been removed but rather, going forward, a single composite Block Grant allocation will be provided to the EA combining the budget allocations for both Schools and Youth services.  This will bring Youth funding into line with other established education budgets and will give the EA the flexibility to make appropriate, evidence-based decisions in respect of its budget in totality.  I would emphasise that all spending remains subject to policy, legislation and Departmental oversight, and the statutory requirement to secure adequate facilities for youth activities remains.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the work of the North City Business Centre.
answer_text: North City Business Centre (NCBC) is a registered charity established in 1991 to provide advice, support and accommodation for small businesses in North Belfast.  NCBC seeks to address enterprise creation and development, support skills for employment and act as a catalyst for economic regeneration.  In collaboration with NCBC, I have recently completed a substantial investment project which has brought the total footprint of NCBC to almost 200,000 square feet through three new sites. Following redevelopment, the new sites will attract many new and growing businesses, and create new local jobs in this highly disadvantaged area. This project has been made possible with £3.75m from the Local Growth Fund.  North Belfast has enormous potential, and this acquisition ensures that local people and local businesses will be at the centre of future growth.  I look forward to seeing these buildings become vibrant assets that support opportunity, innovation and community wellbeing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any issues raised through the objections to the current planning application for an incinerator at Hightown, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: This application remains a live case under consideration by my Department. All representations, including letters of objection, are publicly available on the online Planning Portal under reference T/2014/0114/F.In reaching a decision, I must consider all material planning considerations, including issues raised in objections. Therefore, it would be inappropriate for me to comment on any specific planning matters at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any pre-school settings in North Belfast that have requested transition to full-time provision, under the Pre-School Standardisation Programme; and (ii) which of these requests have been (a) approved; and (b) refused, in each of the last two years.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s work to secure a second site for Coláiste Feirste in North Belfast.
answer_text: The Development Proposal approved in November 2020 to increase enrolment at Coláiste Feirste did not provide approval for a North Belfast satellite provision or stand-alone school.  That said, in 2021/2022 my department’s Investment & Infrastructure Directorate undertook site searches in North Belfast and the wider Newtownabbey area to identify suitable sites for a potential new Irish-medium post primary, should a Development Proposal for a new school be published.No suitable sites were identified, and no Development Proposal has been published to establish a new post-primary school.I am aware of the current accommodation pressures at Coláiste Feirste.  To address these, my department is currently considering the acquisition and refurbishment of former Corpus Chrisi school site, which is adjacent to existing school campus.  An Integrated Design Team will be appointed in the coming weeks to develop and assess options.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline her recent engagements in the United States of America.
answer_text: In March, I travelled to the US along with officials from Invest NI, Tourism Ireland, NI Screen and Studio Ulster. The focus of this visit was on economic opportunities from the West Coast of the US. I also travelled to New York where I visited the headquarters of iVerify, a company which is expanding their presence here.The visit focused on strengthening relationships with existing investors, accelerating potential expansions and new projects, promoting the North as a location for large-scale TV and movie production and highlighting the region’s unique cultural and natural assets as a must-see tourism destination.  Our key agencies charged with building overseas markets and business including Invest NI and Tourism Ireland took part in St. Partick’s Day engagements across the Americas including Washington DC.  I attended the opening of Invest NI’s new office location in San Francisco that will enhance Invest NI’s presence in the West Coast. The dedicated team will continue to support our local companies with their exports to the US and promote the North as an attractive investment location for US companies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how projected unmet demand in oversubscribed pre-school settings in north Belfast has been factored into decisions regarding participation in the Pre-School Standardisation Programme.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for engaging with individual pre-school settings and selecting those that will be invited to progress at each stage of the Pre-School Standardisation Programme (PSP). The PSP is concerned with transitioning settings to a full-time session length and with the exception of approved voluntary Amalgamation for Standardisation requests from undersubscribed settings, does not alter the number of funded places offered by individual pre-school settings.The EA Pre-School Education Group is responsible for ensuring that there is adequate pre-school provision in local areas, and to support this, sufficient funding has been provided by my department to meet the projected need for pre-school places for the upcoming academic year. The EA has assured me that there is sufficient provision in the area to ensure that every child in their immediate pre-school year whose parent wishes to avail of a pre-school place can be accommodated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on work to expand Domestic Homicide Reviews to include death by suicide.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline any steps his Department is taking to improve early diagnosis of COPD.
answer_text: Between 2023 and 2025, the Department of Health (DoH) collaborated closely with a wide range of stakeholders across Health and Social Care to design a comprehensive plan aimed at strengthening and modernising diagnostic services for both Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) and asthma. This work was motivated by the growing recognition that earlier and more accurate diagnosis can significantly improve outcomes, reduce avoidable pressures on the wider health system, and support people to manage their conditions more effectively in the community.A key concept for this regional approach came from a successful pilot project undertaken in the Moy area of Armagh. This pilot, which supported seven GP practices and was delivered through the Armagh & Dungannon GP Federation, demonstrated how a structured, locally delivered diagnostic pathway could improve access to testing and reduce delays in confirming respiratory conditions. Insights from this project played an important role in shaping the wider proposal and informed much of the early planning work.Throughout 2025, extensive discussions took place with colleagues across both Primary and Secondary Care to refine the model and ensure it was clinically robust, practical to implement, and aligned with regional priorities. This collaborative effort resulted in an agreed regional pathway for the early diagnosis of COPD and asthma.In November 2025, the newly established Respiratory Forum for Northern Ireland, jointly led by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within DoH and the Public Health Agency, approved the proposed pathway. Their endorsement marked a significant milestone in progressing this work.Looking ahead, DoH will now explore opportunities to secure funding for implementation, subject to the financial settlement for 2026/27 and future years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of (a) special educational needs (SEN) schools; and (b) SEN units within mainstream schools, both primary and secondary, in North Belfast, (ii) whether they are currently operating at capacity; and (iii) to detail anything his Department, and the Education Authority, are doing to support them.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:The EA Special Schools’ Service provides over-arching support to special school leaders and staff, co-ordinating support across health and education on a range of matters relating to staff development, policy implementation, curriculum support, advice and training on resources and equipment. As the service holds budgetary responsibility for special schools, designated officers also work closely with school leaders on financial management and allocation of resources.There is no formal approved capacity for Special Schools. This is subject to change based on the individual needs of the children attending in any given year, to ensure that each child receives the right support for their individual needs.There are currently 44 specialist provision classes operating in the North Belfast constituency across primary and post-primary schools. Specialist provision classes are needs-based, and capacity measurements are more complex than in a mainstream setting. The Education Authority works closely with schools to ensure that children with special educational needs are placed in the most appropriate provision to meet their individual needs. The recommended capacity for classes varies depending on the type of provision; however, class sizes retain a degree of flexibility based on the overall profile of the children placed. There is currently no surplus capacity in post-primary specialist provision and limited surplus capacity within primary school specialist provision in North Belfast. This reflects the high demand for places and flex required within the system to accommodate statutory assessment and review, which may lead to a new/change of place at any stage throughout the academic year, in line with changing pupil needs.  The Education Authority continues to engage with all schools with a view to establishing additional provision to address the needs in local communities.  We remain committed to the strategic vision that all children and young people with SEN receive the best possible education in their own communities, in schools as close as possible to where they live.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment his Department has made of the suitability of Universal Credit processes, for households where both parents work full-time, and rely on childcare reimbursement.
answer_text: The aim of the Universal Credit childcare element is to support parents in low-income families to work, where paid childcare would be a barrier to employment. Universal Credit provides support to households to help meet their eligible childcare costs, supporting claimants to become financially resilient by moving into work or increasing their working hours. Claimants can receive up to 85% of their childcare costs, up to the maximum limit of £1,031.88 for one child or £1,768.94 for two or more children. To be eligible for support with childcare costs claimants are required to satisfy the childcare cost condition and the work condition, unless an exception applies, to qualify for help with childcare costs.The work condition stipulates that both claimants in a joint claim must be in paid work (either employed or self-employed) or have accepted an offer of paid work due to start before the end of the next assessment period or have stopped paid work in the current or previous assessment period. While childcare costs are paid by reimbursement, the Adviser Discretion Fund (ADF) may be used to assist claimants with upfront childcare costs where it is identified as a barrier to the claimant moving towards, obtaining, retaining or progressing within employment or self-employment. Further information on the ADF is available at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/adf.Further information for claimants on potential childcare support is available from the Make the Call Service on 0800 232 1271. This service is there to make sure that claimants are getting all the benefits, services and supports they are entitled to. Further information is available online at www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/make-call-service. My Department is satisfied that the processes in place deliver on the aims of the Universal Credit childcare costs provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether he will publish an impact assessment of how the move from the UK Shared Prosperity Fund to the Local Growth Fund has affected (a) the community and voluntary sector; (b) those employed through the previous scheme; (c) service delivery.
answer_text: As both these funds are delivered by the UK Government, my Department is unable to assess the full impact of the proposed financial allocations under the new Local Growth Fund from April 2026. I am aware, however, that there will be significant impacts, including job losses, on those community and voluntary organisations delivering important services to support those people furthest from the labour market.My Department will continue to deliver a wide range of provision to meet need, from one-to-one support provided by work coaches across my Jobs and Benefits offices, recruitment events, and tailored programmes including JobStart, Access to Work, Workable and the Condition Management Programme, as well as responsive flexible activity funded through the local Labour Market Partnerships and Neighbourhood Renewal programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the timeframe for relocating all remaining patients at Muckamore Abbey Hospital to suitable accommodation.
answer_text: There are currently 7 individuals remaining in Muckamore Abbey Hospital, and resettlement plans are in place for 4 of these to have left the hospital by the end of February.  Planning is continuing to finalise suitable community placements for the other 3 individuals.Members will be aware that work to complete the resettlement of patients from Muckamore is being taken forward by the Regional Resettlement Oversight Board, chaired by Dr Patricia Donnelly.The Board continues to meet on a fortnightly basis and their work is informed by ongoing engagement with key staff from the relevant Trusts, providers of community resettlement accommodation, and patients and their families and advocates.As the hospital population continues to reduce, the staffing position at Muckamore is being kept under ongoing review. RQIA is represented on the Oversight Board and continue to work with the Belfast Trust to monitor on-site services and I understand that the RQIA recently carried out an inspection at the hospital.  We await the publication of their findings.As ever, my focus remains on ensuring that services at the hospital for the remaining patients can continue to be provided safely, and that any contingency arrangements that may be required to facilitate this are in place.The primary purpose of any such arrangements will always be to ensure the safety and wellbeing of the remaining patients.These contingency arrangements are considered at meetings of the Regional Resettlement Oversight Board, and will be discussed with patients and families should they be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the consultation on the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30.
answer_text: On 6 January I published proposals for a draft multiyear Budget and formally launched a public consultation.  The consultation will run until 3 March.In parallel to this, I have been continuing to engage with my Executive colleagues.  Last week I undertook a series of Budget bilateral meetings with Ministers. I am committed to working constructively and in partnership in relation to the Draft Budget to find a way forward.The Draft Budget proposals I have brought forward have been developed against the backdrop of an exceptionally constrained financial position. The proposals therefore do not provide the level of funding I would wish to see directed at our public services; instead, they reflect what is possible within the funding available. In this context it is simply not possible to provide any department with the funding it has requested. I have been clear that adequate funding is required from Westminster for investment in public services. I continue to make this case.However, despite the challenges, the Executive must grasp this opportunity of a multi-year budget to provide certainty and aid long term planning to enable strategic investment that delivers lasting benefits for our economy, our environment and our society.It is essential that we now listen to the voices of families, workers, business and communities to ensure that the decisions we make in agreeing a Budget reflect the needs and aspirations of the people we serve.I encourage everyone to have their say during the consultation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme, including the number of people who have availed of it.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme is a key initiative within the Elective Care Framework, designed to help meet the growing demands for services and ensure that no patient waits longer than a year for treatment.  This Scheme specifically targets those who have been waiting the longest.  While the original eligibility threshold was set at two years, my Department reduced this to one year to broaden access for patients and accelerate progress in reducing waiting times. The Scheme commenced on 2 June 2025 and offers patients who are ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland, and who have been on an HSC hospital treatment waiting list for one year or more, the option to obtain and pay for treatment privately outside Northern Ireland and have reimbursement up to the equivalent average cost Health and Social Care (HSC) cost for that procedure.   Implementation has been phased:  Phase One enabled reimbursement for treatment in the Republic of Ireland (Rol). Phase Two, launched 15 September 2025, extended eligibility to include treatment across the wider European Union. The scheme is operating effectively, with efficient processing times and high patient satisfaction among those who meet the eligibility criteria. From the launch of the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme on 2 June 2025 up to 22 January 2026, a total of 493 applications have been successfully prior-approved to seek treatment outside Northern Ireland and claim reimbursement up to the HSC equivalent cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the actions he will take to ensure that every homeless person is offered accommodation when the Severe Weather Emergency Protocol is initiated.
To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of whether the criteria and checking processes, for those who apply for a firearms licence, are robust enough.
answer_text: Northern Ireland has a robust firearms licensing system under the Firearms (Northern Ireland) Order 2004 (the 2004 Order).  Applications for the grant of a firearm certificate, under Article 4 of the 2004 Order, are made to the Chief Constable.  The practical and day to day application of firearms licensing is, therefore, an operational matter for the Chief Constable.In assessing applications, the Chief Constable is required to be satisfied that the person is ‘fit’ and has ‘good reason’ to be entrusted with a firearm without danger to public safety.  Good reason also needs to be demonstrated by the applicant for each firearm or ammunition holding being sought.Applicants for a firearm certificate must provide two referees, a declaration in respect of all relevant medical conditions and there is a requirement to inform the Chief Constable of any changes in circumstances within the period of the certificate.  Should PSNI be made aware of any concerns regarding an applicant, at any time, their fitness to possess a firearm(s) will be reviewed and their licence may be varied or revoked.  Other actions may include seizing firearms in response to any concerns raised, both in relation to the FAC holder or anyone within the house. If there is an ongoing investigation or court proceedings firearms are also retained by police.Anyone convicted of an offence which results in a custodial sentence of 3 months or more is prohibited from holding a firearms certificate for 8 years; for sentences of 3 years or more, the prohibition is for life.  For the purposes of the Act, a custodial sentence includes a suspended sentence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of recent media reports, whether her Department has given consideration to introducing legislation, to limit the number of firearms and the amount of ammunition an individual can own legally, at any given time.
answer_text: Applications for the grant of a firearm certificate or to vary a certificate, under Article 4 of the Firearms (Northern Ireland) Order 2004, are made to the Chief Constable. The practical and day to day application of firearms licensing is therefore an operational matter for the Chief Constable.In assessing applications, the Chief Constable is required to be satisfied that the person is ‘fit’ and has ‘good reason’ to be entrusted with a firearm without danger to public safety. Good reason will, therefore, need to be demonstrated for each firearm and/or each holding of a quantity of ammunition being sought by an applicant.Whilst my Department does not have any current plans to introduce legislation to limit the number of firearms or the amount of ammunition an individual can own legally, at any given time, it is a matter which we will keep under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to improve the timely investigation of suspected long-term reproductive health conditions, such as those involving recurring cysts or endometriosis-type symptoms.
answer_text: There is currently significant work underway across the health and care system to reduce waiting times and develop pathways for gynaecology-specific conditions within each Trust across Northern Ireland.To support this, the Department has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, which includes representation from Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and General Practitioners.   This forum is overseeing ongoing service improvements and the development of gynaecology service pathways across primary, intermediate, secondary, and tertiary care levels which are clinically led.   It is also responsible for implementing the recommendations of the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report.Most referral pathways begin in primary care, and the gynaecology pathways being developed reflect this.  If a GP is unable to assess or treat a patient locally, the patient may be referred to the Gynaecology Enhanced Primary Care Service—if they meet the criteria—or to secondary care gynaecology services.Primary and Secondary Care teams are working collaboratively to ensure patients are seen in the right place, at the right time, by the right professional.  In addition to accepting referrals from primary care, a pilot initiative is underway to identify patients on secondary care waiting lists who could be managed within the Enhanced Primary Care Service.  Eligible patients will be assessed and treated by one of the 16 specialist GPs with enhanced skills in gynaecology.  This service currently manages referrals for pessary fitting, HRT management, initial assessment and treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding, and contraceptive management including coil fitting.  As of 30 September 2025, the Enhanced Primary Care Service has delivered 1,567 face-to-face appointments and 587 remote consultations at a cost in-year of £1.07m.  When fully operational, it is expected to manage approximately 6,072 patient contacts annually (including both face-to-face and remote appointments).Secondary Care services are also developing action plans to address waiting times and implement relevant GIRFT recommendations within each Trust.  Between April and December 2025, additional non-recurrent funding has been approved of £3.9m to provide waiting list initiatives—both in-house and via the independent sector to address red flag or other time-critical assessments and treatments.  As well as Trusts carrying out administrative validation exercises, to review and cleanse current outpatient waiting lists, clinicians are also carrying out enhanced clinical validation of outpatient lists.  Additional outpatients are also in place to provide one stop clinics for colposcopy, hysteroscopy, smears and pessaries that cannot be provided in primary care.   Trusts have established a Gynaecology Provider Collaborative - February 2025.  As part of this process, two Collaborative sub-groups have been established, namely:   -A Clinical Standardisation Group – with Clinical and Director/Ass

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that conditions like endometriosis can present with cysts and severe recurring pain, to detail what is being done to (i) improve early diagnosis; and (ii) provide additional training for GPs.
answer_text: (i) Improve early diagnosisTo improve earlier diagnosis of patients, the Minister is non-recurrently funding additional activity to reduce waiting times for new general gynaecology (gynae) outpatient appointments in all Trusts to 3 years by    31 March 2026.Diagnosis can be complex but discussions have been ongoing to agree a regional model, taking account of primary, secondary and tertiary care needs.  A number of services are needed, alongside gynaecology; including transvaginal ultrasound scanning, imaging and diagnostic surgery, which makes diagnosis multi-factorial.At this time, patients with such conditions are waiting on general gynaecology waiting lists, as symptoms can be shared with other gynae related conditions.   Most referral pathways begin in primary care, and the gynaecology pathways being developed reflect this. If a GP is unable to assess or treat a patient locally, the patient may be referred to the Gynaecology Enhanced Primary Care Service—if they meet the criteria—or to secondary care gynaecology services.Primary and Secondary Care teams are working collaboratively to ensure patients are seen in the right place, at the right time, by the right professional. In addition to accepting referrals from primary care, a pilot initiative is underway to identify patients on secondary care waiting lists who could be managed within the Enhanced Primary Care Service. Eligible patients will be assessed and treated by one of the 16 specialist GPs with enhanced skills in gynaecology. This service currently manages referrals for pessary fitting, HRT management, initial assessment and treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding, and contraceptive management including coil fitting.  As of 30 September 2025, the Enhanced Primary Care Service has delivered 1,567 face-to-face appointments and 587 remote consultations at a cost in-year of £1.07m. When fully operational, it is expected to manage approximately 6,072 patient contacts annually (including both face-to-face and remote appointments).Secondary Care services are also developing action plans to address waiting times and implement relevant GIRFT recommendations within each Trust. Between April and December 2025, additional non-recurrent funding has been approved of £3.9m to provide waiting list initiatives—both in-house and via the independent sector to address red flag or other time-critical assessments and treatments. As well as Trusts carrying out administrative validation exercises, to review and cleanse current outpatient waiting lists, clinicians are also carrying out enhanced clinical validation of outpatient lists. Additional outpatients are also in place to provide one stop clinics for colposcopy, hysteroscopy, smears and pessaries that cannot be provided in primary care.   (ii) Provide additional training for GPsAs part of the ongoing and regional modernisation and improvement work in gynae services, training sessions will be introduced with GPs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the policies around the restrictions on young women choosing to have an oophorectomy, even when they understand the medical implications.
answer_text: There are no specific policies relating to oophorectomy. Patients will be assessed individually and provided with a personalised care plan based on their needs. Medical teams follow the Trust’s consent policy and adhere to GMC best practice guidelines. Young women undergoing an oophorectomy will have received full counselling regarding the implications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department is (i) developing; and (ii) reviewing, any procedures for managing recurrent cyst ruptures, in cases where patients have required repeated admission to hospital.
answer_text: The care pathway for the treatment of recurrent ovarian cysts includes: Observation – most small, asymptomatic cysts can be monitored without treatment.Watchful Waiting – a policy of watchful waiting is often recommended with ultrasound scans to check for changes.Hormonal Treatments – in some cases, hormonal therapies may be used to prevent new cysts froming.Surgical Options – if cysts are large, persistent or causing symptoms, surgical removal may be necessaryEach Trust has confirmed their pathway for management below:BHSCT Response:Burst ovarian cysts are very common and can occasionally result in acute pain and hospital admission via the Emergency Dept.The main stay of treatment is oral pain relief though a small number of women may require laparoscopic surgery.Prevention of recurrence typically involves medication such as the combined oral contraception pill and select progestogen only pill to prevent ovulation. NHSCT Response:Women experiencing a ruptured ovarian cyst commonly present to the emergency department and are managed through a combination of medical interventions, including analgesia, intravenous fluids and hormone therapy. In certain instances, surgical intervention may be necessary. After treatment, patients are referred back to their Primary Care team, specifically their General Practitioner (GP). The Northern Trust collaborates closely with GPs to offer guidance on prevention and recurrence through medications, such as hormonal therapy. There is no routine surveillance involving scans unless symptoms are present, in which case arrangements can be made through Primary Care. Additionally, there is ongoing development within Primary Care to identify GPs with enhanced clinical skills, known as GPECS. Further information regarding GPECS can be obtained from GPs.SHSCT Response:There is no specific procedure specifically in respect of these patients/presentations.Care is individualised, and surgical options explored according to each individual case.	Presentations of this nature are low in numbers.SEHSCT Response:Cysts are often physiological and therefore by definition can happen in repeated cycles – conservative management and pain relief is the normal care pathway with further intervention if required.WHSCT Response:Recurrent cyst ruptures are rare. Patients will be assessed and an individualised plan of care devised. The Trust is not developing or reviewing any procedures for managing recurrent cyst ruptures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Emotional Health and Wellbeing Team that families are referred to by the Family Support Hub in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, (ii) for an update on when the service will be open for referrals again; and (iii) to detail the waiting time from when a referral has been made to when families receive support.
answer_text: The BHSCT Emotional Health and Wellbeing Team have advised that based on their planning process this year, 195 places were allocated to the Family Support Hubs for the period September 2024- August 2025. The Team subsequently received 330 referrals over the course of the specified period.  The service is not currently “closed” to new referrals, however, all available places for this allocation period have been filled. Of the 135 referrals above capacity, 45 did not meet the criteria for the service, leaving 90 additional referrals. The service accepts a higher number of referrals as experience would indicate that not all families will take up support once offered.  We understand that many families have children attending multiple appointments and difficulties accessing childcare. Due to differing life circumstances at the time of support being offered, families may decline support due to feeling it is not the right time for them.  Families are offered support within twelve weeks of their referral being accepted, some families may be seen sooner than this if cancelled appointments become available.  There are occasions when families are offered an earlier slot but they opt to wait for a later group, closer to the area in which they live or online. All of the families referred through the Family Support Hubs and Health Visitors are offered programme places during the relevant allocation period. The next round of allocations will take place at the end of November 2025 and will run until the summer of 2026.  I hope this response provides clarity about this service.

answer_text: I recognise the significant negative impact that fly-tipping can have on the natural environment and the problems it can cause in rural areas including the Belfast Hills. 	 	Both the NIEA and District Councils have access to various provisions under the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997, enabling them to undertake enforcement action where appropriate.			Fly-tipping incidents occurring on public land are primarily managed by District Councils. The NIEA continues to work in close partnership with local councils to address fly tipping through a working protocol.		Under the protocol the NIEA leads on incidents of fly-tipped waste exceeding 20m³ or when it includes materials such as hazardous waste that Councils cannot process. For amounts exceeding 20m³, or hazardous fly-tipped waste, kindly contact to the NIEA’s Environmental Crime Unit (ECU) by email at environmentalcrime@daera-ni.gov.uk or by telephone at 028 9056 9453.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the availability, and suitability, of outdoor play facilities in schools, in North Belfast.

To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme.
answer_text: In May 2025, I approved a £7m investment in General Dental Services for 2025/26 which includes £2m over the next two years to fund the continuation of the Enhanced Child Examination scheme.The scheme currently involves a one-off payment of £16.44 for each child aged between 0-10 years who is newly registered with a GDS dentist and has an enhanced examination completed, which includes an examination, dental disease risk assessment, individualised oral health advice, and age-specific fluoride application.The Enhanced Child Examination scheme began in June 2024 and in 2024/25 almost 34,000 children were seen under the scheme. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what proportion of GP practices currently operate ADHD shared-care agreements, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: As clinical and legal liability associated with a prescribed medication falls to the doctor who issues and signs the script, a GP must be confident in the diagnosis, support and guidance given to them and the patient, by the recommending specialist.  The decision to enter into a shared care agreement, or not, is therefore a clinical decision for individual GPs to make and as such, is not monitored.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the key delivery from the Urban Villages Initiative in North Belfast.
answer_text: A total of £35.5 million is being invested in 18 capital projects through Urban Villages and partnership funding, driving regeneration and community development across Ardoyne and Ballysillan.Thirteen Capital projects have been successfully delivered, including the £4.3million redevelopment of Marrowbone Millennium Park, along with youth clubs, community centres, play parks, and environmental improvements.Five further projects are progressing, with two transformational builds currently on site with a total investment of £16.6 million: ABC Trust Health & Leisure Hub; and Ballysillan Playing Fields. Under the Urban Villages revenue programme, over £2 million was invested in North Belfast Urban Village community-led projects from 2018-2024, reaching more than 34,000 project participants.Urban Villages is prioritising the completion and sustainability of capital projects, with a strong commitment to building the capacity of local community organisations involved in operating them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light recent remarks by the US President suggesting links between pregnant women taking paracetamol and autism, what actions will (i) his Department; and (ii) the Public Health Agency take (a) to address this misinformation; and (ii) to provide resources to reassure pregnant women that paracetamol during pregnancy is safe.
answer_text: The Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) has confirmed that there is no evidence that taking paracetamol during pregnancy causes autism in children.  MHRA continuously monitors the safety of all medicines, including those used during pregnancy, through robust monitoring and surveillance. All regulatory advice on medicines in pregnancy is based on rigorous assessment of the best available scientific evidence. In my address to the Assembly on Tuesday 23 September, I rebutted the alleged link between paracetamol and autism. I noted that the independent autism reviewer supported this statement. My address was shared on the Department’s social media, as well as the video address by the MHRA’s Chief Safety Officer, Dr Alison Cave, where she noted there is no evidence that taking paracetamol during pregnancy causes autism in children.My Department has circulated the MHRA’s Drug Safety Update Paracetamol and pregnancy – reminder that taking paracetamol during pregnancy remains safe, to the HSC by way of a HSS MD 33 2025 letter available  via the Department’s letters and urgent communications webpage at the following link https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/letters-and-urgent-communications-2025 on 24 September, advising that taking paracetamol during pregnancy remains safe. Healthcare professionals are asked to note and action the advice for healthcare professionals, advice for healthcare professionals to provide to patients, and recommendations contained within the Drug Safety Update. The DSU has been distributed to HSC healthcare professionals in both primary and secondary care settings.Paracetamol remains the recommended pain relief option for people who are pregnant when used at the lowest dose and for the shortest duration. Untreated pain and fever can pose risks to the unborn baby, so it is important to manage these symptoms with the recommended treatment.People who are pregnant are advised that they should continue to follow existing HSC guidance and speak to their healthcare professional if they have questions about any medication during pregnancy.The MHRA has comprehensive systems including the Yellow Card Scheme for reporting suspected side effects, analysis of healthcare databases, and review of international evidence. The Yellow Card scheme safeguards medical products quality and efficacy in the UK. Members of the public, patients and healthcare professionals are encouraged to report any suspected side effect from medicines, including paracetamol, to the MHRA’s Yellow Card reporting scheme, electronically.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department supports newly-diagnosed patients with coeliac disease in accessing dietary services.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, all individuals with a confirmed diagnosis of coeliac disease must adhere to a gluten-free diet.   Gluten-free products are widely available in local grocery stores and supermarkets.  Patients can also access a range of gluten-free products without any charge through a prescription from their GP practice.  The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within my Department continues to recommend that the choice of gluten-free products and quantities prescribed are in line with the guidance produced by Coeliac UK. The guidance can be found at  https://www.coeliac.org.uk/document-library/8983-prescribable-product-list-july-2023/?return=/information-and-support/coeliac-disease/once-diagnosed/prescriptions/ and was updated in July 2023.  It details the range of staple gluten-free products which are available (bread/rolls, breakfast cereals, crackers and crispbreads, flour, flour mixes, oats, pasta, pizza bases).  Items which are not consistent with healthy eating advice such as cakes and biscuits are not recommended for prescribing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of endoscopies conducted for suspected coeliac disease in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Unfortunately, the number of endoscopies undertaken for suspected coeliac disease are not recorded.  Endoscopies are provided for a number of conditions and are not specific to patients with suspected coeliac disease.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many hours of training is provided on coeliac disease in undergraduate medical education.
answer_text: It is not possible to quantify the exact number of hours of training provided to medical students at Queen’s University Belfast or Ulster University on this condition as specific conditions are not taught in isolation and the placement experiences of students will vary. The universities have confirmed that this condition is covered in both medical degree programmes. Students attend specific lectures on this condition and are signposted to textbooks and online learning on the topic.During clinical placements students are frequently exposed to patients who have a diagnosis or are suspected of having coeliac disease.  They will also spend time focusing on a specific case study which includes a number of related differential conditions of which coeliac disease is the core.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether GPs receive mandatory continuing professional development on coeliac disease.
answer_text: After completing undergraduate training, which will include up to date learning about gastroenterology problems including coeliac disease, GPs engage in continuing professional development (CPD) and will address their own learning needs through participation in online and in person courses, reading journal articles, implementing new clinical standards and guidelines, engaging in audit and quality improvement. Much of the ongoing learning and professional development is accessed through the various professional bodies. Within primary care, training can be accessed locally through GP Federations.  The content of training delivered by GP Federations is considered and chosen by members of the Federation in keeping with the training needs locally and regionally identified by our health care professionals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how encompass has impacted (i) patient care; and (ii) Emergency Department operations since its introduction.
answer_text: For the first time in history, all HSC Trusts across Northern Ireland are now integrated into one digital system, signifying a major step forward in streamlining patient care.encompass is a major transformational change with an ultimate goal to empower patients and improve their access to care. Used well, encompass will deliver important improvements to care quality and productivity, ensuring staff have access to health information and supporting them to deliver safe and effective care. Following their successful Go-Lives, feedback has been received from members of staff in Northern, Belfast & South Eastern Trusts showing that patient care has already been positively impacted by the change.Examples of testimonials from staff have reported benefits of having increased collaboration across the Trust with the introduction of mobile tools like Rover streamlining community documentation and enhancing governance of care regardless of location. Community staff have also noted a clearer overview of patients’ health needs and ongoing treatments, with immediate access to other relevant professionals’ notes, ensuring better continuity of care. This has been particularly beneficial in bridging social and clinical care.Moreover, the ability to review patient charts from anywhere has expedited patient care and decision-making. Improved communication pathways between acute and community services have led to better resource utilisation. Appointment booking processes have also improved, preventing double bookings and ensuring smoother service delivery.With encompass now fully operational across Northern Ireland, following Southern & Western Go-Live on 8th May, all patients and service users will be able to securely access a subset of their health and social care record, such as clinic letters, medication details, and some test results via the My Care patient portal. They can also view appointments and track the progress of their treatment.  My Care is currently providing secure online access to personal health information to approximately 130,000 users. In terms of Emergency Department operations, staff have embraced the new system and are using it to effectively deliver patient care.  Data from the system is being produced from encompass and published in the Urgent & Emergency Care Waiting Time Statistics for NI report for South Eastern, Belfast and Northern Trusts across all core areas including attendance statistics and activity data such as triage levels, reattendances, and performance against waiting time targets.  encompass will work with Southern & Western Trust and other key stakeholders to ensure data is produced now that all Trusts are live on the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting time for children's (i) autism; and (ii) ADHD assessments in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Belfast Trust has now gone live with Encompass, the new electronic patient record system. Their reporting is undergoing extensive validation. There is currently no validated information available at this time in relation to paediatric autism waiting times. As attention deficit hyperactivity disorder is not commissioned regionally it is not consistently reported through Encompass and Belfast Health and Social Care Trust have been able to manually provide this information; at 31/03/2025 there was 1,227 children waiting. Trusts manage waiting lists based on clinical and chronological order, in line with the Integrated Elective Access Protocol (IEAP). ADHD assessment is defined as a routine request under IEAP and therefore HSCTs define ‘present wait’, ‘Current wait’ or ‘the wait’ as the ‘longest wait’ as this will be the time the next child referred can expect to wait for an initial appointment. The current longest wait for an initial assessment for a child referred this week is approximately 267 weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the (a) nature; (b) frequency; and (c) outcomes of any engagement he has had with other Departments in the last 12 months in relation to the integration of suicide prevention objectives into their respective policies, action plans and budgets; and (ii) how his Department is (a) monitoring; and (b) supporting the implementation of these commitments.
answer_text: There was extensive engagement with other Departments, to refine the actions and agree ownership and responsibilities during the development of the new action plan and implementation plan for Protect Life 2. These plans were discussed and agreed at the Executive Working Group on 3 April subject to funding. Once published the Department and the Public Health Agency will be developing enhanced monitoring arrangements for the Strategy to track progress and develop an outcomes framework with a wide range of indicators to assess progress. Discussions have already taken place with other Departments in this regard and to identify appropriate data. My Department recognises that the impact of the Strategy is much wider than solely rates of suicide and self harm and is keen to reflect the vast number of outcomes which contribute to improving mental health and wellbeing, suicide prevention and self-harm across Departments.A wide range of actions have been agreed with other Departments to ensure their contribution to the Protect Life 2 Strategy and commitments given to support this work which recognises this is a cross departmental issue.

To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to his commitment to reduce health inequalities, how he will ensure that this commitment translates into measurable, sustained improvements beyond pilot projects such as Live Better.
answer_text: Health inequalities are one of my areas of focus as Health Minister. Given the drivers of health inequalities are often outside the direct control of my Department, addressing this issue will require sustained collaboration across NI Executive Departments and Agencies, local government, the community and voluntary sector, and with communities themselves to address the factors which impact on health and wellbeing locally and regionally.Making Life Better (MLB) is the overarching strategic framework for public health through which the Executive committed to creating the conditions for individuals, families and communities to take greater control over their lives and be enabled to lead healthy lives. The next stage of MLB is currently being considered, taking into account the many recent developments in this space, whilst also considering the need for the most effective and efficient use of existing resources.MLB is underpinned by a range of public health strategies and policies which focus on specific issues, such as substance use, suicide prevention, tobacco use and obesity prevention. These policies also have a focus on health inequalities and are implemented on the ground by the Public Health Agency. In addition, health inequalities have been identified as a key issue for the Integrated Care System NI and for the new Area Integrated Partnership Boards. Live Better is a new approach designed to help address health inequalities by bringing targeted health support to communities which need it most. It is currently being tested in two areas. Importantly it is being fully evaluated and, should it prove to be effective, this process will inform the future development and mainstreaming of the Live Better approach for the benefit of all communities across Northern Ireland.Live Better is referenced in the Programme for Government and will be reflected in the next phase of MLB.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the £61 million allocated for the expansion of the Multidisciplinary Team Programme, to detail (i) how much will be specifically allocated to support the roll-out in North Belfast; and (ii) how that funding will be distributed across local GP practices.
answer_text: The Primary Care Multidisciplinary Team (MDT) Programme is a partnership between GP Federations and HSC Trusts which introduces new physiotherapy, social work and mental health roles into general practice, to work alongside the existing practice teams.  The MDT Programme remains key to stabilising and strengthening vital Primary Care services and the confirmation of £61m of Transformation funding will see the MDT Programme brought to completion in the 7 GP Federations which have already commenced and expansion into an additional 5 GP Federation areas with a combined population of 670,000. (i) It is the planning assumption within the Transformation Bid that around £2.5m would be invested across the 5 new areas in 2025-26. This amount will increase year on year in line with agreed implementation plans. (ii) Detailed plans are currently being developed between Trusts and GP Federations at a local level to determine the sequencing and timescale of investment in practices. SPPG will not be able to confirm opening allocations/levels of investment available for each area until all local plans are received and reviewed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any feedback that has been received from patients about their experience with Mega Clinics; and (ii) how this feedback is informing future developments.
answer_text: Mega Clinics help to maximise patient throughput, reduce waits and are key components of the Health and Social Care (HSC) reform agenda.  They offer significant benefits to the HSC as they alleviate pressure across the system by targeting large cohorts of patients in a ‘one stop shop’ and can be rolled out across multiple specialties and enable effective use of the available workforce by consolidating elements of the patient pathway e.g. outpatients, diagnostics, pre-operative assessments and even treatments.  Feedback from patients has been positive with patients noting the convenience of being able to see their consultant and anaesthetist on the same day. This will inform further development of this model across the region. Constant evaluation and learning will allow improvements and refinement of the Mega Clinic model. However, expanding Mega Clinics can only happen if there is sufficient staffing and resource to enable them to do so and this will require recurrent funding. Unfortunately, my transformation bid for funding to further develop Mega Clinics was not progressed by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how the Fuel Poverty Strategy will address immediate fuel poverty concerns while working toward long-term energy efficiency goals.
answer_text: While the draft Fuel Poverty Strategy is focused on long-term, sustainable solutions to fuel poverty, it also recognises that people will still experience immediate concerns and crises when access to emergency financial support will be required. Therefore, the draft strategy proposes an action to work with other organisations to ensure consistent emergency support and referrals to energy efficiency measures and advice.To address immediate fuel poverty concerns, my Department funds the Northern Ireland Housing Executive to deliver the Affordable Warmth Scheme. The Scheme assists low-income owner-occupiers and households in the private rented sector, with an annual household income of less than £23,000. To make an application to the Affordable Warmth Scheme, customers can contact the NI Energy Advice Service via: 0800 1114455 or email: NIenergyadvice@nihe.gov.uk My Department provides a Winter Fuel Payment Scheme, which is an annual payment of either £200 or £300 (depending on age) made to pensioner households in receipt of Pension Credit or other means tested benefits to help with heating costs.Following the change to the eligibility for Winter Fuel Payments, a one-off £100 Pension Age Fuel Support Payment will be made to pensioner households in Northern Ireland who are no longer eligible for the Winter Fuel Payment.Financial support is also available through Cold Weather Payments which are triggered when the average temperature is, or is forecast to be, zero degrees centigrade or below for seven consecutive days, from 1 November to 31 March each financial year.  My Department’s Make the Call Wraparound Service continues to support individuals and families across Northern Ireland who otherwise may be missing out on accessing benefits, services and support they are entitled to.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how the network of defibrillators is managed locally; and (ii) who is responsible for (a) upkeep; (b) accessibility; and (c) the publishing of all accurate information on its times of availability.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) leads on the implementation of the Community Resuscitation Strategy and is funded to provide a supporting role in advising organisations, businesses, and individuals regarding siting of defibrillators and best practice for defibrillators. NIAS has worked with the British Heart Foundation to develop ‘The Circuit’ (https://www.thecircuit.uk/) which is a national online defibrillator network linking to every ambulance service in the UK.  NIAS can readily provide information on AEDs registered on ‘The Circuit’ to 999 callers, should they require access. Currently there are approximately 3708 permanently fixed community defibrillators registered with NIAS on the ‘The Circuit’. Further information on the location of defibrillators can be found at www.defibfinder.uk or by contacting the NIAS Community Resuscitation Team at resus.admin@nias.hscni.net or telephone 028 9040 5215.All Public Access Defibrillators (PADs) should be registered on The Circuit. Part of this process involves assigning a guardian. Both NIAS and The Circuit encourage registering multiple guardians, this decreases the chances of emails being missed or overlooked. The Circuit’s registration process involves inputting the hours of availability of the defibrillator to the public and notes the precise location of the AED. This includes the address, the location of the AED at the address and the option to drop a pin on the map provided on The Circuit. The responsibility for maintenance, upkeep and checks of PADs then rests with the registered guardians of the defibrillator. Regular, automatic emails are sent to each guardian from The Circuit to check their defibrillator. The checks involve ensuring the defibrillator is serviceable, pads are in date, battery is in date and the emergency ready kit (razor, scissors, towel & face shield) is stocked. If a defibrillator is deployed by the ambulance service, the guardian  will receive an automated email asking them required to perform checks and log into their Circuit account and return the defibrillator to emergency ready.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any lessons from the Mega Clinic pilots in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and the Western Health and Social Care Trust that can be applied to future elective care initiatives.
answer_text: The introduction of Mega Clinics is helping to maximise patient throughput and reduce waits. The concept of Mega Clinics is a key component of the HSC transformation agenda.  Mega Clinics offer significant benefits to the HSC as they alleviate pressure across the system by targeting large cohorts of patients in a ‘one stop shop’ and can be rolled out across multiple specialties.  They also enable effective use of the available workforce by consolidating elements of the patient pathway e.g. outpatients, diagnostics, pre-operative assessments and even treatments.  Joint surgical and anaesthetic preoperative assessment Mega Clinics have been piloted across Belfast and Western Trust areas, bringing together surgical, anaesthetic, nursing and administrative teams to enable assessment of patients who have been waiting a long time for surgery.   In many cases, patients leave the clinic with a date for their procedure at one of the regional elective care centres within three months of assessment. These Mega Clinics have provided positive feedback from multidisciplinary teams and patients alike and will inform further development of this model across the regionHowever, Mega Clinics can only proceed if there is sufficient staffing and resource to enable them to do so and sustainable expansion of the mega clinic model will require recurrent funding. Unfortunately, my transformation bid for funding to further develop Mega Clinics was not progressed by the Executive.In the meantime, I will continue to do all I can, in a very constrained financial environment, to ensure that we can expand successful and transformative projects like this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how community pharmacies have been prepared and supported financially to address the increased workload expected with the roll-out of Pharmacy First Service for Sore Throat (Winter 24/25).
answer_text: Following a successful evaluation of the Pharmacy First Pilot Service for Sore Throat last year, and collaborative discussion with Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI) DoH has secured £1m non-recurrent funding for a network wide service that commenced on 1st December 2024 and will run for two months in the first instance.Mandatory training on Service Provision, Antimicrobial Resistance and on the Clinical Management of sore throat was provided by SPPG at a live ECHO training event at 7.30pm on Tuesday 5th November 2024. This training event was recorded and is available on the ECHO Moodle site for those unable to attend the live session.  The fees payable to pharmacy contractors for this service are:One off service set-up payment of £200 per pharmacy contractor A consultation fee of £32 per consultation. This fee includes the cost of Rapid Antigen Detection Tests and any other necessary consumables.The cost of medicines supplied will be reimbursed on submission to BSO of the prescriptions or pharmacy vouchers.Work continues between SPPG and CPNI to implement the agreed 3-year Commissioning Plan for 2022-25 with associated funding and builds on the successful commissioning agreements that have been in place over the past years. This ensures that community pharmacies across the region provide a range of services to meet the needs of patients and HSC.
