I will bring the strategy to the Executive once consultation responses have been analysed and the draft strategy has been updated to reflect those responses.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide further detail on the coordination between his Department, the PSNI and Belfast Harbour Police in identifying and disrupting illegal breeders.
answer_text: The PSNI and Harbour Police are key members of my Department’s Paws for Thought initiative, which aims to disrupt the breeding and trafficking of low welfare pups.  Under this initiative, my Department has developed better ways of working aimed at targeting known individuals who are moving consignments of pups through the two main ferry ports, to Great Britain, with the support of the PSNI and Harbour Police.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the measures his Department will take to address illegal puppy farms.
answer_text: Unlicensed commercial breeding of pups is a criminal offence and local councils are responsible for enforcing the legislation. The maximum penalty for breaching the law is a six-month custodial sentence and / or a fine of £5,000. My Department, through the “Paws for Thought” group, is conducting a public facing awareness campaign to highlight the role everyone has to play in tackling the illegal puppy trade. Additional communications are currently ongoing throughout December in a bid to raise awareness of the abhorrent illegal trade and ensure that potential owners seeking a new puppy in the run up to Christmas do so responsibly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the courts take into consideration the impact on the welfare of an animal and its owner when determining penalties for pet abduction cases.
answer_text: Sentencing decisions are a matter for the independent judiciary who come to their decisions after consideration of all the evidence presented in the case, as well as any relevant sentencing guidelines and sentencing principles.However, I am advised that whilst the legislation does not make specific reference to factors to be considered by the judge, the Policy Background in the Explanatory Notes refers to a specific pet abduction offence providing “greater recognition of the impact on the animal, in addition to its owner, when it is unlawfully taken” when deciding penalties, rather than simply the financial loss.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any safeguards and exceptions that are included in the Pet Abduction Act 2024 to ensure those carrying out innocent actions, such as helping what they believe are stray pets, are not criminalised.
answer_text: The Act does not seek to criminalise someone who is genuinely acting in the best interests of a stray dog or any pet. It provides discretion to the courts to determine on case-by-case basis whether the taking or the detention of the animal is unlawful or there is a reasonable excuse. This is to ensure that well-meaning behaviour is not inadvertently criminalised.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how her Department is addressing potential trends in the number of deaths in custody, including suicides or cases involving drugs.
answer_text: Any death in custody is a tragedy and I would like to extend my sympathy to any families who have been affected by the death of a loved one in prison.Since 2016, there have been 45 deaths in prison custody in Northern Ireland:1 death has been confirmed at inquest as drug related;2 potentially drug related (7%); 7 have been confirmed as suicide;12 potentially self-inflicted (42%); 4 have been confirmed as natural causes;18 potentially natural causes (49%); and1 death has been found undetermined at inquest (2%). In all deaths in custody the appropriate authorities are notified of the deaths.  The Prisoner Ombudsman takes forward investigations into the deaths and the cause of death is determined by the coroner following an inquest. The Prisoner Ombudsman makes recommendations for both the Northern Ireland Prison Service and the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust following a Death in Custody investigation. Deaths in custody impact not just on the families of those individuals but also on other prisoners and prison staff.  Support is provided as necessary after each incident.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what specific measures are in place to support vulnerable prisoners, including those with mental health or addiction issues.
answer_text: Responsibility for health and social care of prisoners in Northern Ireland sits with the Department of Health who have commissioned South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (the Trust) to provide primary, mental health and addictions services within prisons. Many people arrive in prison with complex needs. Support is available for people who have additional needs, including those who have been confirmed by the Trust as having mental health and addiction issues.  This includes access to mental health, addictions, psychology, learning disability and speech and language staff within the Trust’s Healthcare in Prisons Team. A joint approach between NIPS and the Trust to support people who may be at risk of suicide and self - harm is well embedded across all prison sites, together with environmental changes, access to specific activities, garden spaces and therapy animals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the increase in early onset cancers such as colorectal, bowel and breast cancer in those under 50 years old, whether there are plans to expand or adjust the current cancer screening programmes to include younger age groups.
answer_text: The UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC) which advises the four UK Health Departments on all aspects of screening, does not currently recommend screening for bowel and breast cancer in those under 50 years old. Research is underway to assess the effects of offering breast screening to women aged 47-49 years old. The findings will inform the advice of the UK NSC to Ministers on the age that breast screening should be offered. The Department will continue to be guided by the recommendations of the UK NSC. However, as with all screening programmes, screening is for people without symptoms. People who are concerned about, or are experiencing any unusual symptoms, should seek advice from their GP.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline how much additional funding the Interim Fiscal Framework has secured for public services.
answer_text: In total, the application of the 124% needs-based factor from the Interim Fiscal Framework has resulted in an additional £431.4 million of additional funding for the Executive.  This demonstrates that the Interim Fiscal Framework, which I agreed on behalf of the Executive in May, has and continues to deliver for people by providing hundreds of millions of pounds in additional funding for public services hereI am in regular contact with my Ministerial counterparts in the Treasury, pressing on the need for a fair funding settlement for the North, including agreeing the mechanics of the application of the needs-based factor going forward, the calculation of our relative funding per head, and the approach to reviewing our level of need.I met with both the Chief Secretary to Treasury and the Chancellor in mid-July. Then, along with the First and Deputy First Ministers, I met with the Chancellor on 12 September. I also met the Chief Secretary at the Interministerial Finance Committee on 3 October alongside counterparts from Scotland and Wales, where I had a direct bilateral meeting with him. In all of these engagements, and in separate correspondence, I continue to push for the full implementation of the commitments within the Interim Fiscal Framework.We will continue to move at pace to deliver our commitments as set out in the Interim Fiscal Framework.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the timeline of the Together: Building a United Community review.
answer_text: The T:BUC Strategy was launched in 2013 and since then considerable progress has been made in key thematic areas such as shared housing, creating urban villages and engaging young people. Five of the headline action targets in the Strategy have been met or will be met in the near future, while progress continues to be made in complex areas such as interface removal. But we want to build on this. We want to create thriving communities which are equipped to tackle the many challenges facing them today.  That’s why we asked officials to conduct a review of the Strategy with the intention to develop a refreshed approach to Good Relations, to ensure we best meet the needs of everyone who calls here home. 	A final draft of the Together: Building a United Community review is currently under development by officials, and recommendations are expected to be submitted shortly. The recommendations of this review will inform the timeline for the development of a refreshed approach to Good Relations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to improve the recruitment and retention of social workers.
answer_text: My Department has worked collaboratively with HSC Trusts, Universities and Colleges, trade union representatives and other key stakeholders to try to alleviate some of the pressures brought about as a result of recruitment and retention issues in HSC social work teams. A Social Work Workforce Review was undertaken in 2021 and its findings were published in 2022. The Department then established the Social Work Workforce Implementation Board, involving senior leaders and a range of workforce representatives, to plan and deliver real improvements in the short, medium and longer term. Considerable progress has been made in alleviating pressures and improving stability in the social work workforce, including:in December 2022, the decision was taken to end the use of recruitment agency social workers in all HSC services by June 2023.the Trusts then recruited around 160 agency social workers into vacant HSC posts between January and June 2023 and all usage of agency social workers in HSC has now ceased. the Department created a faster, more efficient route into Trust employment for newly qualified social workers, which resulted in 208 vacancies being filled between June and September 2023. So far in 2024, a further 204 newly qualified social workers have begun working in HSC services.the Department has also increased the number of training places for student social workers by commissioning 25 places with the Open University NI between 2021-2023 and has added an additional 40 places in 2024. This brings the total number of social work training places to 325, the largest number on record.The Department continues to make the case for an increase in training places for student social workers, which is entirely dependent upon the availability of additional, recurrent funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action being taken to assist people in North Belfast (i) to get an appointment with a GP; and (ii) to get an appointment with an NHS dentist.
answer_text: I recognise that being able to access GP practices when we need to do so is important for all of us. In response to increasing patient demand GPs are utilising a mixed consultation model with telephone consultations, video and face to face consultations as a means of managing the rising demand on services within the existing workforce while retaining responsiveness to those patients who need the most urgent attention and care. With regard to individual GP practice arrangements, as independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  This also means that decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce. Additional measures have been put in place to support primary care to adapt flexibly to increasing demand.  These include focusing on increasing capacity to meet the demand from patients through, for example, reinforcing the GP workforce and increasing the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care, investment in general practice and the implementation of new and innovative ways of working, including making greater use of technology and telephony.  As part of supporting work in this area, my Department established a GP Access Working Group, which includes GP and service-user representation, to explore issues around accessing services and to oversee work to improve the experience for patients.  This includes work to develop guidance for practices in management of patient demand and optimising workflow within practices to maximise efficiency and consideration of how technology can be best used to improve experience for both patients and practices.In relation to getting an appointment with an NHS dentist you will be aware that access to Health Service dentistry is currently very difficult, especially for unregistered patients, and this is likely to be the case for some time. General Dental Practitioners are independent contractors and, unlike General Medical Practitioners, are under no obligation to register new Health Service patients and are able to decide which patients they will treat under Health Service arrangements. They may also de-register their existing Health Service patients with three months’ notice. Those who wish to register with a dental practice as a Health Service patient are advised to contact practices in your area directly. They may have to check with many dental practices and / or be prepared to travel outside of their local area, in order to find a practice that is currently registering Health Service patients. Further advice, including the HSC Service Finder, can be found at: Registering as a Health Service dental patient - DOH/HSCNI Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG)As part of a £9.2m financial invest

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the expected completion date for special educational units being constructed at Thornfield House Special School, Newtownabbey; and (ii) the resources being provided to children currently missing out on full-time education as a result of these delays.
answer_text: Building works to create two classrooms at Thornfield House completed on 24 September 2024, with internet connectivity being installed the week of 7 October. From 9 September pupils have been engaged in ‘getting to know you sessions’ at the school three days a week.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any specific policies and strategies his Department plans to implement to achieve environmental sustainability and reductions in greenhouse gases in the agri-food sector.
answer_text: My Department’s new Farm Support and Development Programme is being co-designed with the Northern Ireland agricultural industry and other key food and environmental stakeholders.The overall aim is to transition to a more sustainable farming sector by seeking to implement policies and strategies that benefit our climate and environment, while supporting our economically and socially significant agriculture sector.The planned schemes and measures, for example, the Farming with Nature Package and Beef Carbon Reduction Scheme, will provide levers to contribute to statutory obligations under the Climate Change Act and other environmental pressures.My Department is seeking to ensure that as many of the new policy interventions as possible help to drive down the agricultural industry’s carbon footprint.Farming for Carbon will focus on actions to reduce carbon emissions and, by association, other emissions from farms and on actions to offset carbon emissions through sequestration. Carbon footprinting is an important action that farmers can undertake to measure the GHG emissions from their farm businesses and identify where improvements can be made.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the roll-out of encompass.
answer_text: The programme has successfully launched in both South Eastern Trust in November 2023 and Belfast Trust in June 2024. Both trusts are in a stabilisation phase.Lessons learned are being carried forward and preparations are underway for Northern Trust to ‘Go-Live’ on 7th November 2024 with Southern and Western Trusts scheduled for 8th May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he is taking to ensure the Cancer Strategy is fully implemented.
answer_text: My Department has established cancer governance structures to oversee the implementation of the Cancer Strategy. Departmental Officials have been working closely with Cancer Programme Board and Steering Group members to ensure that Cancer Strategy actions remain on track for delivery. There is undoubtedly more to be done, and while some of this work can be completed using the funding and resources currently available, additional investment is required to fully implement the cancer strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, further to the absence of the Police Ombudsman, to provide an update on the Police Ombudsman's Office.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 3 - AQO 872/22-27			ANSWERThe Police Ombudsman remains absent from the Office. I can advise that she has delegated her powers to senior officials in the organisation, as permitted under the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 1998, to ensure that the vital work of the Office continues. I met recently with the Chief Executive and the Senior Director of Investigations, and I am assured that the work of the organisation continues in line with its statutory duties.The Ombudsman is appointed by Royal Warrant on the recommendation of the First Minister and deputy First Minister, and any matters relating to her employment are for the Executive Office to consider.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any long-term strategies being developed to manage and mitigate blue-green algae in bodies of water; (ii) any work he is undertaking with his counterparts in the south to address blue-green algae, given waterways are interconnected; and (iii) whether an all-island blue-green algae strategy will be developed.
answer_text: The Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan, which was approved by the Executive on 18 July 2024, sets out actions to tackle blue-green algae blooms in Lough Neagh and to secure longer-term improvements in water quality in bodies of water across Northern Ireland. The Action Plan is a detailed, science-led, proportionate and ambitious set of 37 actions that will make a real difference to our waterways.   I am committed to driving the Action Plan forward as quickly as possible.Given the transboundary nature of our waterways, success in tackling blue-green algae and water quality issues will depend on collaboration at Ministerial, official and operational levels. At a Ministerial level I have discussed these issues, including cooperation, with my Irish Government counterparts at recent North South Ministerial Council Plenary and Environment Sectoral meetings. In addition, knowledge and expertise are being actively shared, with my officials participating in, for example, the North South Water Framework Directive Coordination Group, the Border Region Operational Committee and the Irish National Technical Implementation Group.  Operational staff also share information on blue-green algal blooms in cross-water bodies to allow appropriate advice to be given to the public.The specific development of an all-island strategy on blue-green algae has not been discussed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action his Department is taking to support community pharmacies.
answer_text: My predecessor, Minister Swann, increased funding to community pharmacy bringing the opening position for community pharmacy to £147m this year, an increase of £19m from the opening position of 2023/24. This is the highest level of funding to date in NI, which compares favourably to the rest of the UK, and represents a 40% increase in funding over the last 6 years.My Department recognises the fundamental role that community pharmacies have been carrying out in delivering health services to communities in Northern Ireland and has repeatedly acknowledged the concerns raised by Community Pharmacy NI (CPNI), and by individual contractors themselves in respect of drug pricing increases and differences due to inflationary and cost of living increases generally.The Department is facing a significant funding gap to maintain existing services. The HSC continues to face an extremely unpredictable and volatile position. There are significant difficulties in terms of financial planning across the HSC and officials are continuing to work closely to review the position as we move forward. However, whilst we will continue to keep the position under review with a view to containing costs as much as possible, you will appreciate that significant changes may give rise to further pressures having to be managed in year. My Department recognises the valuable role community pharmacy has within health and social care and I am happy to report that a Strategic Plan for Community Pharmacy 2030 was launched in May 2024. It is available at:  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/community-pharmacy-strategic-plan-2030 The Strategic Plan for Community Pharmacy aims to fully realise the potential of community pharmacy services to support better health outcomes from medicines and prevent illness. Implementation of the strategy will not only support elderly and vulnerable patients but the whole population.This important piece of work was developed collaboratively by the Department of Health and Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI), the representative body for community pharmacy owners, and describes the vision and strategic priorities for community pharmacy up to 2030.My Department will continue to work with CPNI and the pharmacy profession to progress a phased approach to implementation of the Strategic Plan over the coming years subject to funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to expand the JobStart 50+ scheme following the pilot phase.
answer_text: There is currently no funding available to expand the JobStart 50+ Scheme beyond the pilot phase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the metrics or criteria that will be used to assess the success of the magnetic pouch pilot programme; (ii) the outcomes that will determine whether this approach will be expanded; and (iii) when his Department will evaluate the outcomes of this programme.
answer_text: The specifications for the pilot are currently being developed by officials, along with an evaluation framework to help assess the outcomes of the pilot.  Whilst the metrics are to be developed, I would expect the pilot scheme to consider the impact of inhibiting devices on pupils’ engagement with learning, participation in school life, behaviour and well-being.   As part of this process, it will also be important to gather views of school staff on the practical deliverability of the approach and feedback from pupils and parents on their own experience.   It is currently envisaged that the pilot programme will begin early in 2025, with an evaluation completed following the end of the 2024/25 academic year, however the exact timings are dependent on a number of factors.The findings of the evaluation will help inform future policy decisions in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any consultation that has taken place with parents and guardians, students and youth organisations on his new guidance on the use of mobile phones in schools; and (ii) how his Department plans to ensure that students develop digital skills and responsible phone usage habits.
answer_text: (i) The guidance provided to schools is informed by relevant evidence that highlights the impact of the unauthorised use of mobile devices in school, as well as the excessive use of such devices more generally.   The guidance sets out a range of different approaches that could be used in schools to restrict the use of mobile phones, this includes the potential use of inhibiting devices.  The circular asks schools to revise existing policies accordingly to develop approaches that work for their own environment. As part of this, it is highlighted that schools should consult with the wider school community, including children and young people as well as parents on the specific delivery of policies at school level.(ii) The integration of digital skills into all areas of the Northern Ireland Curriculum is important both to individual learners and wider society. Equipping students with digital proficiency is imperative for their future success. My department has prioritised digital skills as an area of focus within the curriculum and is working with colleagues in CCEA to develop actions which aim to enhance and integrate digital skills across all areas of the curriculum at all key stages. The curriculum also outlines that pupils should be provided with opportunities to develop knowledge and understanding of e-safety and acceptable online behaviour and the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations Assessment (CCEA) has developed a number of digital wellbeing resources which are available on its website.Furthermore, my department funds the Safer Schools app that provides a digital library of age-appropriate safeguarding resources for children and young people.  I also recently wrote to schools to highlight the Safeguarding Board’s Online Safety Hub (SBNI Online Safety Hub). Over the coming months, my department will consider the wider use of mobile technology for educational purposes and review current circulars on this matter, such as DE Circular 2016/26 Effective Educational Uses of Mobile Digital Devices and DE Circular 2016/27: Online Safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any lessons his Department have taken from other countries that have influenced the development of the magnetic pouch pilot programme; and (ii) any way which his plan differs from other countries' approach.
answer_text: Mobile inhibiting devices used to restrict the use of mobile phones in schools is an approach that is used internationally, including in the USA, England and Republic of Ireland.  I am aware that, where these devices have been used, there have been positive outcomes in terms of pupil engagement, attainment and behaviour.  However, to evaluate this specific approach further, I have asked Departmental officials to develop a pilot project which would enable the strengths and weaknesses of this approach to be considered in the local context. This development is in the early stages, and initial engagement with schools will take place in the coming weeks. Whilst the pilot and evaluation framework will be designed with the specific needs of Northern Ireland schools in mind, officials will also take account of any learning available from the wider international experience.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline her plans to address the rising number of hate crime incidents.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 13 - AQO 749/22-27 – WITHDRAWNANSWERI note with concern that the recent statistical data on hate crime indicates a significant rise in racist hate crime and hate incidents in Northern Ireland. Hate incidents and hate crimes can have an equally devastating impact on their victims. Whilst hate incidents do not involve a criminal offence, victims of hate incidents are nonetheless often subjected to behaviour which is frightening and disempowering for victims and their families. Addressing hate and prejudice requires a cross-government approach and the DOJ is working with other Departments to develop more cohesive and inclusive communities where diversity is welcomed and valued. Working with other departments is critical, especially on the development of preventative actions to help challenge the attitudes and behaviours that enable race hate crimes. This work can help to reduce and prevent hate crimes happening in the first place.There is a range of support mechanisms available to victims of hate incidents and hate crimes including the Hate Crime Advocacy Service which offers a comprehensive range of support to victims. My Department also supports PCSPs whose work includes awareness raising and education initiatives on reducing hate crime within local communities such as providing bilingual advocacy services and hate crime prevention sessions.I am also finalising proposals to the legislative programme to ensure that foundational legislation to deal with hate crimes, which can lead to real improvements for victims, will be progressed within the current mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when the single-use plastics directive will be transposed into domestic law.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the joint initiatives undertaken with the Minister of Education to improve mental health support in schools in the last two years.
answer_text: A continuum of services has been developed to support the implementation of the Framework for Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education (2021).  This framework is a collaboration between The Departments of Education and Health.  Each service within the framework has been developed to complement the others and to ensure the needs of children and young people, their families and school staff are met. Within the framework a number of health-led programmes are underway:Emotional Wellbeing Teams in SchoolsEmotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools (EWTS) is a Children and Adolescent Mental Health (CAMHS) pilot programme.  Since September 2023, the pilot has been dedicated to supporting a number of identified post primary schools at any one time for one academic year.  In academic year 2023-2024, 46 schools have been supported.  In year 2024-2025, the capacity of schools EWTS can support is 68.  EWTS works in the schools to promote the positive psychological, emotional and mental health of children. This includes building individual and collective resilience and supporting capacity within educational staff by equipping staff with the knowledge, skills and ability to provide intervention at the earliest opportunity for children and young people with emerging emotional and mental health concerns.  The overall aim is for a reduction in the growth of longer-term mental health difficulties for children and young peopleText-a-NurseText-a-Nurse is led by the PHA.  This service provides a secure and confidential text messaging service for young people aged 11-19, which allows them to easily and anonymously get in touch with a school nurse for advice and support they may need. The service was extended in February 2023 to enable access to Text-a-Nurse by parents/carers and education staff who have concerns about a young person and wish to seek advice. The Text-a-Nurse service reflects the strong focus and value of promotion, prevention and early intervention through which health, educational settings and community services can work in an integrated way to support the child or young person both within the school setting and where required with wider family support services.Enhanced School NursingThe Enhanced School Nursing project is also led by the PHA.  The school nurse is located in a school, with a reduced caseload, to deliver innovative, transformational and pupil focused services. There are certain groups of children more likely to experience poor mental health including; Looked After Children (LAC); children of ethnic minority origin; children living in areas of high deprivation; children living with disabilities and illnesses; LGBTQ children; and children in contact with the criminal justice system.  The activities of school nurses have included; increased numbers of school aged LAC receiving a holistic health appraisal in the school setting; supporting emotional health and wellbeing of pupils to maintain school attendance and impr

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) the implementation of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031; and (ii) its key aims.
answer_text: Delivery of the Mental Health Strategy has been taking place within an extremely challenging financial context using existing, limited resources to take forward actions. As a result of this, the Strategy is being implemented through Annual Delivery Plans which set year-on-year priorities and actions. While the number of actions which can be taken forward are limited due to the funding position, good progress has been made in taking forward a number of actions, including:Implementation of the community perinatal mental health service across the region, with teams now in place in all Trusts;Co-design of a five-year implementation plan for the establishment of the Regional Mental Health Service (RMHS) and appointment of the Head of the Service; Completion of a three-year Early Intervention and Prevention Action Plan, with progress made on implementation of actions;Completion and publication of an independent Mental Health workforce review, with work underway to begin planning implementation of key recommendations;   Additional investment in Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) to enhance existing provision and alleviate pressures;Roll-out of the emotional health and wellbeing teams in schools, with initial cohort of schools identified and engaged;Establishment of the Digital Mental Health Board and appointment of project leads to develop a digital mental health service action plan; and  Completion of a review exploring  approaches to engaging the community and voluntary sector in the development of mental health policy and provision.The aim of the Mental Health Strategy is to set out a clear direction of travel to support and promote good mental health, provide early intervention to prevent serious mental illness, provide the right response when a person needs specialist help and support, as well as outlining how the system will work to implement these changes.The Strategy sets out 35 actions under three overarching themes:Promoting mental wellbeing, resilience and good mental health across society;Providing the right support at the right time; andNew ways of working.The Mental Health Strategy and Delivery Plans for Years One and Two are available at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/mental-health-strategy-2021-2031.  The Delivery Plan for 2024/25 is currently under development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action his Department is taking to promote employee ownership.
answer_text: Question 3 - AQO 642/22-27Employee ownership has the potential to increase productivity and create fairer workplaces. However, currently, the North has only a limited number of employee-ownership businesses and I would like to see that increase.I have directed my Department to undertake research on employee ownership.  This will include an examination of the advantages or disadvantages of employee-ownership as a business model, a consideration of case studies of businesses that have become employee-owned businesses and a look at the potential for growth of employee-owned businesses. The research will be published in the latter part of summer 2024.Invest NI are also already working with the business sector, including the social economy sector, to promote employee ownership for business succession planning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when his Department will publish the review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants.
answer_text: My officials are currently finalising the modelling of the options for the review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants.  This is to reflect the latest data from the Department for Communities that takes account of wage rises and the ongoing transition of some legacy benefits claimants to Universal Credit.I plan to launch a public consultation on the options for changing the eligibility criteria in the new academic year.I fully recognise that both free school meals and uniform grants can provide genuine help to low-income families. The provision of free school meals also enables many children to access a nutritious meal which in some cases may be their main meal of the day. This brings with it a range of both physical and mental health benefits as well as the educational benefits for children being in the best position to learn. It was in this context that this review has been examining options for how more pupils can access these invaluable supports.However, in the current budgetary position, a key issue in this review that we cannot simply ignore is affordability. My Department is facing significant funding pressures and is struggling to support existing key services.  There are potentially significant budgetary implications of increasing the numbers of children eligible for free school meals and I will need to take these implications into account before taking any final decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 12104/22-27, whether any learning from other countries who have successfully implemented clean air strategies has been applied or will be applied to his Clean Air Strategy.
answer_text: My department has reviewed previous strategies, and the recent work of other jurisdictions across the UK with appropriate learning from other administrations considered and applied in development and preparation of the NI Clean Air Strategy. On specific matters we also have worked closely with the Republic of Ireland and Department for the Economy on restrictions on solid fuel, and with England on particulate matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the resources that are available to summer schemes for children with special educational needs in the Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council area.
answer_text: The department does not directly fund summer schemes for children and young people with special educational needs (SEN). Youth service provision seeks to be inclusive of all young people who choose to participate.  Details of any youth service summer provision in Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council area can be sought directly from local youth organisations.School based summer provisions have, historically, been supported through the Education Authority(EA) block grant across 21 of the 39 special schools, due to the profile of children and young people attending the school.  Within Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council area, this has included summer provisions at Hill Croft and Riverside schools.  Since 2021, due to the changing profile of needs, the department has provided additional funding to the remaining 18 schools to deliver summer provision for their pupils.  Rostulla and Thornfield House schools have requested funding for Summer 2024. The provision of out-of-school hours activities for children and young people with SEN, disability and complex medical needs is under consideration as part of the End to End (E2E) Review of SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the support provided in schools to pupils with dyslexia; and (ii) the specific support provided to these pupils during exams.
answer_text: Where Dyslexia/Specific Learning Difficulties in Literacy have been identified within an Education Authority (EA) psychology assessment, the Educational Psychologist will recommend to the school a range of strategies and resources after considering those previously used by the school.  These targeted recommendations form part of the outcome of the psychological assessment and may involve a referral to the EA Literacy Service for either advisory support or direct teaching intervention. A range of additional resources including information, guidance on accessing training, suggested activities and resources to help parents and schools to support literacy difficulties where they arise or are at risk of arising can be found at https://send.eani.org.uk/support-services/literacy.Schools and parents can also contact the Literacy Service to seek advice, guidance and information for any pupil, either referred to the service or not, by phoning 028 9448 2228 during normal office hours.  Alternatively, the service can be contacted by e-mail at literacyservice@eani.org.uk.The End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) and associated workstreams are considering all options for reform across SEN policy areas to ensure that children and young people with SEN have the right support from the right people at the right time in the right place.  Access arrangements allow candidates/learners with SEN, disabilities or temporary injuries to access the assessment without changing the demands of the assessment.  The details of how this can be accessed are found at https://www.jcq.org.uk/exams-office/access-arrangements-and-special-consideration/.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant AQW 11139/22-27 to detail (i) the Departments involved in the cross-departmental working group; (ii) each Department's role in developing the Irish language strategy; and (iii) when he expects the working group to issue the action plan.
answer_text: All departments have appointed a representative to the Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture and Irish Language Cross-Departmental Working Group with the exception of the Department of Health.As part of the co-design process, the Cross-Departmental Working Group’s role is to support DfC in the development of the Irish Language Strategy and action plan, along with that for the Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture Strategy. I am currently considering the next steps in the development of the Irish Language Strategy and the Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture Strategy and the associated action plans. I will then engage with Executive colleagues to ask them to consider and if content agree on those next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on her discussions with Treasury on a fiscal framework.
answer_text: I recently signed an interim Fiscal Framework with the British Government. The Framework clarifies a number of elements of the financial package. For example, the 24% needs-based factor will be applied to all new Barnett consequentials since the restoration of the Executive. This is worth £24 million to the Executive. I have also obtained the agreement of the British Government that the needs-based factor will apply this year and next, even if the funding from the financial package is above the assessed level of need.The British Government have also committed to reviewing the approach to the funding of the Executive before 2026-27. The Framework states that the Executive can plan on the basis it will be funded at or above the 124% level of relative need in future financial years. This provides assurance that the Executive will not face a cliff-edge once the financial package ends. The Framework also includes a commitment that the 124% level of relative need will be reviewed if a range of sources can be provided to support a change.I intend to move quickly to begin engagement on a final Fiscal Framework once the General Election takes place.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their plans to implement best practice arising from the Communities in Transition programme.
answer_text: To date Communities in Transition (CIT) has delivered a total of 82 projects with a value of £19 million across the eight CIT areas. Fifty-two of these projects have been completed, with 30 contracts extended into 2024/25.The project is currently delivering community resilience interventions across the following eight areas:Carrickfergus and Larne (Antiville and Kilwaughter in Larne together with Northland and Castlemara in Carrickfergus);Derry/Londonderry (Brandywell and Creggan);North Down (Kilcooley & Rathgill);Lurgan/Craigavon (Drumgask and Kilwilkie);   West Belfast (Lower Falls, Twinbrook, Poleglass, Upper Springfield, Turf Lodge and Ballymurphy);North Belfast (New Lodge and Greater Ardoyne); Shankill (upper and lower, including Woodvale), andEast Belfast (The Mount and Ballymacarrett).Our officials have recently completed a review of phase 1 and 2 delivery of the programme and intend to utilise the learning and good practice identified to inform future delivery. Through the review it has been determined that the most serious issues impacting communities as a result or paramilitarism and organised crime are:The impact of paramilitarism on health and wellbeing;Vulnerability to exploitation: monetary, sexual and child criminal Vulnerable youth: lack of aspirations, educational disadvantage, and positive choices; andThe impact of paramilitarism on the physical landscape.This year CIT has already refocused delivery to address these most serious issues impacting communities through a more targeted approach.A few examples of good practice interventions in North Belfast which we plan to build on moving forward into any new phase of delivery are:The establishment of a social supermarket which helps those living in poverty who may turn to illegal money lenders to get back on their feet The Buddy Brigade was set up to support a group of young men and women from North Belfast. The aim being to help them understand the value of money, that not everyone pretending to be your friend is a friendly person, the pitfalls of gambling and also what they should do if they feel that someone is not genuine; and             ‘Youth Realities on Stage’ a series of awareness raising plays used to improve awareness of bullying, child sexual exploitation, knife crime, domestic abuse, child criminal exploitation and drugs.Our officials will commission research to look at the prevalence of paramilitarism across the CIT areas and beyond to inform any future delivery plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) what steps his Department is taking to support the Housing Executive in making upgrades to homes; and (ii) any upcoming schemes in North Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department's plans for social and affordable housing in North Belfast.
answer_text: There are 517 social homes programmed to start in the North Belfast parliamentary constituency in the 2024/25 - 2026/27 Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP), which was approved in early 2024 by my Department. The construction of new social homes will be dependent on the available budget.  I have already acknowledged the challenging budget situation faced by my Department, and my disappointment that the capital funding does not exist to build more social homes.  I have allocated an additional £15million to the SHDP for the coming year, although I accept this is a very significant reduction on previous years.Please note that programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval. Additional schemes can also be added to the SHDP ‘in-year’ through Competitive Land, Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / Off-the-Shelf properties.Planning teams within each local Council are responsible for assessing the future housing requirements for their Council areas. These assessments include affordable housing requirements and are reflected in each Local Development Plans (LDPs) which are at various stages of development and implementation. The Northern Ireland Housing Executive is responsible for preparing a Housing Need Assessment for each of the Local Development Plans. Currently £158 million of funding has been provided to Co-Ownership to support over 4000 households into homeownership by 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of GP practices in North Belfast that have handed back their contracts in 2023-24.
answer_text: My Department can confirm that there were no GP Practice contract hand backs in North Belfast in 2023/24.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will fully fund the teacher pay award, taking account of (i) back pay; (ii) the annual increase on school budgets; and (iii) the recurring cost incurred in the three year financial plans that schools are currently completing with the Education Authority.
answer_text: As part of the HM Treasury Financial Package, my Department secured funding to meet the cost of the teachers’ pay arrears.  This funding has been allocated to the Education Authority and schools will be allocated the full cost of the arrears, in due course. All departments’ opening budgets for 2024-25 were agreed at the end of April by the Northern Ireland Executive.  I will endeavour to protect the Aggregated Schools Budget as much as possible; this includes taking account of recent pay uplifts.  However, the budget outcome for Education falls far short of what the sector requires.  Given the size of the funding shortfall faced, I am working with my Department’s officials to determine the options available and what difficult decisions are required to offer the education sector the best chance to operate within budget.  Schools will be notified of their 2024-25 final opening allocations in due course. The number of years covered by the Executive’s budget depends on the period covered by the Treasury’s spending review.  As 2024-25 is the last year of the current spending review, and the Executive’s funding beyond this review period has not yet been confirmed, I am unable to provide the assurance your query seeks regarding future years’ budgets. However, I will continue to strongly advocate for the additional investment our schools need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what he is doing to increase capacity to ensure all children are suitably placed in a school in time for the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: My department works with schools to ensure sufficient capacity exists in areas and school sectors where oversubscription arises.  This has been done proactively, through the allocation of additional places to post-primary schools by way of the Year 8 Additional Places Exercise, and in response to demand as it arises at both primary and post-primary level, through the Temporary Variation process.The primary school admissions process has concluded for 2024-25 and almost 21,000 children secured a place with only 16 children still to be placed.  Parents of these children may now apply to schools with places available and almost 7,000 places remain available across Northern Ireland.  The post-primary admissions process concludes on 18 May.Children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) (40% of all children with SEN) follow a different process for admission to schools, which is based on consultation between the Education Authority (EA), schools and parents and seeks to ensure that their school placement is appropriate to meet their needs as set out in their statement and which is subject to change at the annual review of their statement.  There is a continual flow of children being referred for SEN assessment and each child is treated on an individual basis.The EA has overall responsibility for special education provision in Northern Ireland and is the lead planning authority  Meeting the demand for specialist education places for September remains a significant challenge, and the EA, supported by my Department, is working at pace on a significant programme of work, supported by my Department, to create additional specialist education places in both special schools and specialist provision in mainstream schools (SPiMs) for the 2024/25 academic year.  A joint taskforce group has been established and meets weekly.  There has been a very positive response rate to a recent survey issued to all schools seeking that they consider the establishment of new or additional specialist position and a programme of school engagement is underway to scope deliverable options for September.  My department and the EA, in collaboration with managing authorities and sectoral bodies, are working closely to identify what steps need to be taken to get ahead of this year-on-year reactionary response to increasing demand, and to ensure appropriate forward planning in line with projected need.

Once agreed, it will issue to me for consideration and shall then issue to the Executive to consider for public consultation. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to domiciliary care packages, how many hours per week on average are not provided against the agreed total in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust are funded to provide 32,100 hours per week from the Independent Sector.  Over the last financial year, they have provided between 40,500-43,000 hours per week.  The Trust are therefore delivering more hours than they are funded for at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department has to address the level of NHS dentistry services in North Belfast.
answer_text: I recently announced a £9.2m investment in dental access initiatives for the 2024/25 financial year.  This investment includes £1m to fund an Enhanced Child Examination Scheme, £4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for priority treatments, and £3.9m to fund a Dental Access Scheme for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients.  In addition, a 6% pay uplift for dentists will be back-dated to April 2023 and is in line with the recommendation from the Doctors’ and Dentists’ Review Body.  The uplift comes with a 7.3% uplift to non-staff expenses, with a 3% recurrent award in future years and represents an investment of £7m for 2023/24 and £5.3m for future years.  These investments aim to bolster support for dental practices and protect public access to health service dental care across Northern Ireland including in your local consistency of North Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether he has reviewed the outcomes of the Healthy Happy Minds counselling pilot; and (ii) whether his Department has any plans to launch similar pilots for children in primary schools.
answer_text: I will take the time to assess the findings of the evaluation along with wider research as well as feedback from stakeholders in developing the Department’s longer-term approach to supporting children and young people’s emotional health and wellbeing in education.There has not been a core budget for delivering counselling services to children in primary schools within the Education budget.  Any future counselling service, funded by the Department of Education, would be subject to budgetary resources being available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department is working with community and voluntary sector organisations that offer mental health services to ensure comprehensive support.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency works closely with the community and voluntary sector in relation to mental and emotional wellbeing and suicide prevention key areas of work include: PHA Quality Standards for Services Promoting Mental and Emotional Wellbeing and Suicide PreventionPHA, with support from the CLEAR project, manage the Standards which are designed to create a quality framework within which any service providing mental and emotional wellbeing support and suicide prevention across Northern Ireland should operate. They focus on setting minimum standards and improving governance arrangements within organisations to provide safe and effective practice for service users.All organisations, including community and voluntary sector organisations who are supporting people with their mental and emotional wellbeing or engaged in suicide prevention should complete the core standards. These set a minimum standard of service, practice and improve outcomes for service users. Depending on the nature of the projects, it may also be useful for organisations to complete some or all of the ‘Additional Standards’. These provide specific frameworks on:TrainingSelf-harm servicesCounsellingComplementary therapiesBereavement support servicesBereavement support groupsOrganisations are encouraged to undertake a self assessment, however each year, a number of organisations are chosen at random for independent assessment. More information about the Quality Standards can be found at PHA - Welcome - PHA Standards Assessment Tool (pharesourcehub.co.uk)Mental and Emotional Health and Wellbeing and Suicide Prevention Training Framework. PHA’s Mental and Emotional Health and Wellbeing and Suicide Prevention Training Framework was developed in coloration with stakeholders from across the statutory, community and voluntary and private sectors. The Framework is in line with Northern Ireland’s Mental Health Strategy and Protect Life 2, and guides learners through the Tiers and types of training to support knowledge and skills development. Training is commissioned across Northern Ireland through Trusts and Community and Voluntary sector. This training is available free of charge to employees and volunteers of Community and Voluntary sector organisations. PHA Mental and Emotional Health and Wellbeing and Suicide Prevention Training Framework | HSC Public Health Agency (hscni.net)Commissioned ServicesPHA commission Community and Voluntary Sector Organisations across the region to deliver services related to key area for Mental Health Promotion and Suicide Prevention. This includes:Early InterventionTrainingCommunity Suicide PreventionSupport for at risk groupsSelf-harmPostvention

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on their preparations for a Programme for Government.
answer_text: Work to develop a Programme for Government is moving forward quickly. The Executive is agreed on priorities, including: childcare; reducing hospital waiting lists; tackling violence against women and girls; Special Educational Needs; housing; developing a globally competitive economy; and Reform and Transformation of public services. We will move at pace to agree a programme and bring it before the Assembly for scrutiny and approval. We will keep Members updated as progress continues.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the provision of a bus service to (i) Hightown Road; and (ii) Hydepark Road.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline what measures it has taken to provide family and carer friendly working hours for Assembly staff.
To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department has to reduce waiting lists for adults and children awaiting ADHD assessments.
answer_text: Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals there is currently no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The limited capacity available in Trusts in terms of meeting demand and the level of prevalence of child and adult ADHD is leading to individuals waiting longer than assessment and support than described in NICE guidelines.The Department of Health are committed to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet NICE compliant pathways for ADHD diagnosis and support.Department of Health officials are considering how a future ADHD service might be delivered to meet needs, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years.  In line with the usual process for commissioning services, any decision to commission ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail plans for investment in podiatry services in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: In recent years, additional funding has been provided to Podiatry services as part of improvements in the rheumatology care pathway. Currently there are no plans for additional investment in podiatry services in the Northern HSC Trust.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their priorities now the Executive has been restored.
